channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.eurogamer.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.eurogamer.net

item.title: Hiring has begun for a new sequel, Tera 2, as Krafton eyes Unreal Engine 5 to secure a potential MMORPG hit
item.content:encoded:
Hiring for key roles has begun for Tera 2 at Krafton, a sequel to the action-oriented MMORPG which shuttered its servers in 2022.

Through its subsidiary studio Bluehole Studio, roles including senior client programmer and client programmer lead have appeared on the Krafton website, indicating development moving past initial design phases.

The game will be created in Unreal Engine 5, and will retain the open action focus of the original MMO. There does not appear to be a mention in job postings of generative AI, despite Krafton's CEO's prior statements on the developer and publisher being an "AI-first company".

Krafton, while known more widely globally as a publisher of games like PUBG, The Callisto Protocol, and Subnautica 2 (though not without legal shenanigans), started out as the developer of Tera. The game was a big hit at the time, popularising the action game style combat as opposed to traditional tab-targeting action. While servers sadly closed down in 2022, senior staff like chairman Chang Byung-gyu have continued to express interest in Krafton having a big MMO under its belt.

It's certainly ramping up development of Tera 2 at an interesting time. Many other MMORPGs have been cancelled outright, be it Amazon Game Studio's Lord of the Rings MMO or the Horizon MMO. What games are doing well are legacy titles, like Old School RuneScape or Eve Online. On top of all this, wider live services struggles remain challenging to studios big and small.

Related topics


https://www.eurogamer.net/tera-2-hiring-krafton-mmorpg-unreal-engine-5
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Assassin's Creed Hexe loses another game director, and it's the second one this year
item.content:encoded:
Assassin's Creed Hexe game director Benoit Richer has announced his departure from the project, making him the second director-level member to leave the game's development team this year.

As spotted by Rock, Paper, Shotgun, Richer announced that he had left Ubisoft to join Quebec-based indie studio Servo Games, where he will serve as co-founder and game director alongside other Ubisoft veterans Luc Tremblay, Danny Marcoux and Alex Droun

"This is the beginning of a new chapter," the now-former Assassin's Creed Hexe developer said. "Proud to contribute to the Quebec creative ecosystem and help it grow." Richer did not go into detail about his new project, though promised more details to come.

Richer's departure from Assassin's Creed Hexe comes just two months after it was revealed that creative director Clint Hocking was also leaving the project. "We sincerely thank [Clint] for his vision, creative contributions, and dedication over the years, and we wish him the very best in his next chapter," an Ubisoft spokesperson said in February, before assuring that work on Assassin's Creed Hexe "continues with a seasoned team" of developers. "The game will deliver something distinctive within the Assassin's Creed franchise. We look forward to sharing more information in the future," Ubisoft said.

Jean Guesdon - head of content for the Assassin's Creed brand - was subsequently appointed the project's new creative director, and has reportedly since gotten "rid of everything that was 'magical' in Hexe". According to internet tipster @xj0nathan, "we were supposed to control a cat" in Hexe, but "ultimately that's been cancelled" since Guesdon has come on board.

But, Richer and Hocking are not the only two developers to leave Assassin's Creed Hexe. Back in October, former franchise lead Marc-Alexis Côté also left the project and Ubisoft. Côté went on to state he "did not walk away" from the company, but was asked to "step aside" by Ubisoft. In January, it was reported Côté was suing Ubisoft following his departure from the company.

As for Hexe, well, this particular Assassin's Creed project still remains largely a mystery. In 2022 when the project was first announced, it looked to be inspired by European witchcraft. We don't yet have a release date for the game.

But, while we may have a while to wait for Hexe, Assassin's Creed Black Flag is getting a remake, which after months of leaks is finally releasing on 9th July. Assassin's Creed Black Flag Resyned, as this remake is known, is set for PS5, Xbox Series XS, and PC.

Related topics


https://www.eurogamer.net/assassins-creed-hexe-game-director-departs
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
